speaker: interviewer
text: This is the family of Albert Roland, survivor. It's March 9, 1997, being taped in Denver, Colorado, United States of America. It is being conducted in English. Mr. Roland, would you introduce your family to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my pleasure. To my left is Bernie Roland, my older son and his lovely wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laurie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Laurie. And the oldest daughter, Hillary, and Michelle. To my right is my wife, Mary Roland. Next to her is sitting Teddy Roland, my youngest son, with his wife Nancy. And this is my oldest granddaughter from Ted, Chelsea Roland, and the younger one, Talia Roland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Manya.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's Manya Rosicki, my older brother's wife-- the deceased one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any-- would any of you like to give your impressions of what it was like, Ted and Bernie maybe, of what it was like growing up with parents who were survivors of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [INAUDIBLE] Well, having both parents that survived the Holocaust was a moving experience. Throughout our life, we were told about the things that happened. And I guess the one thing that I would like to say is, through a combination of luck, foresight, and whatever else you might want to say, we're all here due to the fact that my dad was fortunate enough to survive. So that's about all. Anybody else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your dad ever tell you about his experiences when you were young? At what age did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, throughout my life at, you know, ongoing times, he would talk about various experiences that he had and about leaving Poland, going to Russia, and the struggles that he had there in an effort to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about grandchildren? Did grandpa ever talk about Holocaust? Would anyone like to tell about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Various times during the Holocaust memory week or whatever, the day, my grandpa and my grandma would come to my school and talk to the kids because my school isn't much of a Jewish school and he would just educate them about what it was like to be Jewish and living in Poland at that time. So it's mostly where I get my first-hand experience of it. Anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like, as far as I can remember, like at family gatherings whenever we'd get together, my grandpa or my grandma would tell me stories about how they came over from Europe, and what it was like, and when they got married and everything during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did a big project through fourth and fifth grade about the Holocaust. And I interviewed a lot of people, including my grandpa, and that was probably the most interesting one. And then, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that being born an American Jew, and my parents and their parents also being American Jews, I never would have experienced first-hand the Holocaust survivors. Certainly we knew about it, but it was not a part of our lives growing up, first-hand experience. And also that we're all here today, and that yesterday was Manya and Av's 50th wedding anniversary. I think that's important to tell too, that they've made it this long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I know Al when he was six-year-old. With his two brothers I went in the same grade in school, and I knew his father. But they were a very intelligent family-- a singing family. Every Friday, they would-- you know, they active-like people, the parents, his parents. So that's-- and I survived [INAUDIBLE] Russia, like him, so that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Manya? Mary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went through so-- at the end of the war, we met and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, yet. And on the way back, we got [INAUDIBLE]. When we were in Germany, we got married in 1947. So what can I tell you? 50 years, we're here. And Al will tell you about his life later. That's, you see, we're a beautiful family, beautiful grandchildren, daughter-in-laws, sons, and we're here. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: When Manya and Av came to the girls' school to tell about their experiences during the Holocaust, it was a moving experience for me. A-- and a very proud experience to have a mother and father-in-law who have struggled so hard and come so far and have made the life that they've made for themselves and for their children, and as a result, for us. And I'd just like to say I'm very proud to be part of this family. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And thank you all for being here today. I really appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I appreciate. And I would like to thank my family for being here and express their own opinions. And it was very nice to have something, you know, that we can, for the future generations, that they can talk about it and, and be-- and have the experience. And also a tape to show what happened, what took place in my life. And up-to-date so people will know and be informed what can happen, what history can bring to [BACKGROUND NOISE] people which was close to extinction. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this picture? Who's in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the family from my mother. And the first thing is my mother is on the right-- on the bottom right in the white blouse. Her name is Toba Rosenblatt. Next to her is her father. His name is Pinkus Schbiegel. He was a [INAUDIBLE]. And next to him is his wife, my-- my grandmother. Her name is Rosa Schbiegel. Next, on top row on the left-hand side is my mom's older sister whose name is Freidel, or Frieda, Schbiegel. And next to her is her brother. His name is Itzya Schbiegel. And next to him on the right upper corner is the younger sister of my mother. His name is Pessa Pfeffer, from her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when do you think this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo must have been taken in 1921 or 1920, either way.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. In Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I obtained this photo after the war. I wrote-- there was another sister of my mother's that lived in Switzerland, and I wrote a letter to her. So she was happy to send me, because I told her I have nothing left from-- not even a picture from my whole family. So she sent me this picture--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the, from their family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This photo, please. Tell about this photo, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is a photo of my parents, my father and mother, that was made in 1913 in Lodz, Poland. And that was shortly after they got married-- probably, oh, about seven, eight months after they got married. And there's a signature and a date on the top left side, top corner on the left side, from my father. But he didn't write the year, just the date, the month and the date, and the place where it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is a Hebrew document, an original from my parents. And it's written in Hebrew, which I cannot read it. But it's very valuable to me because it's the original document.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you obtain it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother got it, I don't know from where-- my brother, Aaron. And he had it, and after he died, his wife, little Manya, gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This document is a marriage license. It's a civil marriage license. The other one was a religious marriage, marriage license. And it's done a few years later than the previous one, because if you wanted to legitimate the children, they didn't recognize a religious marriage. It had to be done in a civil manner. So it's done in 1922, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you obtain this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way. My sister-in-law had it after my brother, older brother, died, Aaron, so she gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself. I was, I believe, about 10 or 11 years old, with a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My is in the, in the cap, that four-corner cap, sitting down on the chair. And then my friend who's standing up, his name is Moisha Frish. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --made in Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in 1928 or '29.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made in our backyard where we lived in Lodz. In-- and the pictures, up front, is my middle brother, Henry. Next to him is my cousin, Brunya Schbiegel. Next to Brunya is my mom, Toba Rosenblatt. And the last one, that's me, Avrum Rosenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're the one on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken about, or how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 16 years old. This picture was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made in Russia after my oldest brother, who's in the middle, he came back from labor camp in Russia. And that was made in Shimla. On the left-hand side, that's, is me, Avrum Rosenblatt. The next one, the middle, is my older brother, Aaron Rosenblatt. And on the right side is my middle brother, Henry Rosenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you obtain this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This we took with us from Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all three survived, yeah. But they're not alive anymore. They died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my wedding picture. That's me and my wife, Mary. As you can see, she's on the left-hand side in the white dress with the flowers. And I'm on the right-hand side, standing, posing, you know, after the ceremony-- marriage ceremony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were, where were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947. That was made in Stuttgart, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Mary Zapillar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our, our marriage license, a civil marriage license. And this is done a year later, because we had our religious ceremony and license in Hebrew done in 1947 when we got married. And since we had a son, they didn't recognize it as a legitimate child, so we had to go through that civil marriage a year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in [INAUDIBLE] Wachendorf in 1947. I had to make a correction because I said Stuttgart before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was from-- where was this issued in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was issued in, in Stuttgart, a year later. This picture was done in Stuttgart when my son was one-year-old, Bernie, and I'm holding him up. It was in the back yard on the Gaussstrass in Stuttgart, Germany, in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was done in Stuttgart in 1949 when my son was one-year-old. And that's my wife and I and Bernie, Bernard Roland.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was taken what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the day we left Stuttgart, Germany. And we were already in the, in the railroad car, and I'm looking out of the window saying goodbye to my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to USA, America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a naturalization certificate. I became a citizen of the United States of America in 1957. And this was done in Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what name is, is used on here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I changed my name from Rosenblatt to Roland. It's easier to spell. I had a problem spelling Rosenblatt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made when my older son Bernie became bar mitzvah, and that was done in 1961 in Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who all was in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is, on the left to right, is Bernie Roland, Mary Roland, my wife, and my younger son, Ted Roland, and myself. This-- this picture was done when my younger son Ted became bar mitzvah in Denver, Colorado, in 1963. And there's, from left to right, is Bernie Roland, my older son, Ted Roland, my younger son, my wife, Mary Roland, and myself, Albert Roland. This picture was done in 1988. My brother Henry, which lived in Australia, was visiting us, so we took him to Las Vegas. And also my oldest brother in the middle, Aaron Rosicki. On the left-hand side, is Albert Roland, myself. In the middle is Aaron Rosicki, my oldest brother, and on the right-hand side is Henry Roland from Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is his name Rosicki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he changed his name in Poland. For some reason he wanted to sound Polish because of the anti-Semitism in Poland. So he changed it from Rosenblatt to Rosicki, which sounds Polish.
